figures.caption: Table 1: Synthetic d = 5 results under three-layer leaky-tanh MLP mixing. Entries are mean ± s.d. over five training seeds. Bold highlights the identifiable condition (Distinct ratios); the other two conditions are controls. GMR is omitted for the dense linear family because sparse graph recovery is not the target there.

Figure 1: Latent disentanglement in the nonlinear d = 5 setting (three-layer leaky-tanh MLP mixing). Each column shows the 5 × 5 scatter matrix of true coordinates zj (horizontal) versus learned coordinates z̃i (vertical) for one regime condition. Distinct ratios yields the expected nearpermutation alignment; the controls do not recover a clean one-to-one alignment.

Figure 2: Structural diagnostics for the same setting and run as Figure 1. Top row: mean absolute encoder Jacobian |Dφ| for φ = h ◦ g; near-monomial structure indicates inversion up to permutation and scaling. Bottom row: learned versus ground-truth drift graph, with correct edges/absences in blue/gray and spurious/missed edges in red/pink. Distinct ratios achieves an exact graph match; both controls introduce graph errors.
Table 3: Summary of notation.
Table 4: Simulation and training hyperparameters. All values are shared across the three drift families unless noted otherwise.

Table 5: Simulation results on d = 5 under a three-layer MLP with LeakyReLU activations. All entries are mean ± standard deviation over five seeds. Bold highlights the Distinct ratios condition; the other two conditions are controls. GMR is omitted for the dense linear family because graph sparsity is not the target there.

Table 6: Simulation results on d = 7 under a three-layer MLP with leaky-tanh activations. All entries are mean ± standard deviation over five seeds. Bold highlights the Distinct ratios condition; the other two conditions are controls. GMR is omitted for the dense linear family because graph sparsity is not the target there.

Figure 3: Latent recovery in the dense linear d = 5 setting (three-layer MLP, leaky-tanh mixing). Each column shows the 5× 5 scatter matrix of true latent coordinates zj (horizontal) versus learned representations z̃i (vertical) for one regime condition. Under Distinct ratios, each z̃i aligns with exactly one zj . Both controls fail to disentangle.

Table 7: Simulation results on d = 7 under a three-layer MLP with LeakyReLU activations. All entries are mean ± standard deviation over five seeds. Bold highlights the Distinct ratios condition; the other two conditions are controls. GMR is omitted for the dense linear family because graph sparsity is not the target there.

Figure 4: Mean absolute encoder Jacobian |Dφ| (φ = h ◦ g) for the dense linear d = 5 setting. All three regime conditions use the same representative seed. A near-monomial matrix confirms successful inversion of the mixing under Distinct ratios. Both controls yield less monomial, more mixed encoder Jacobians.

Figure 5: Latent recovery in the dense linear d = 7 setting (three-layer MLP, leaky-tanh mixing). Layout follows Figure 3. Under Distinct ratios, each z̃i aligns with exactly one zj . Both controls fail to disentangle.

Figure 6: Mean absolute encoder Jacobian |Dφ| (φ = h ◦ g) for the dense linear d = 7 setting. All three regime conditions use the same representative seed. A near-monomial matrix confirms successful inversion under Distinct ratios.

Figure 7: Latent recovery in the sparse linear d = 5 setting (three-layer MLP, leaky-tanh mixing). Each column shows the 5× 5 scatter matrix of true latent coordinates zj (horizontal) versus learned representations z̃i (vertical) for one regime condition.

Figure 8: Structural diagnostics for the sparse linear d = 5 setting. All three regime conditions use the same representative seed. Top row: mean absolute Jacobian |Dφ|. Bottom row: learned causal graph versus the ground-truth graph.
Figure 9: Latent recovery in the sparse linear d = 7 setting (three-layer MLP, leaky-tanh mixing). Layout follows Figure 7.

Figure 10: Structural diagnostics for the sparse linear d = 7 setting. All three regime conditions use the same representative seed. Top row: mean absolute encoder Jacobian |Dφ|. Bottom row: learned causal graph versus the ground-truth graph.

Figure 11: Latent recovery in the nonlinear d = 5 setting (three-layer MLP, LeakyReLU mixing). Each column shows the 5× 5 scatter matrix of true latent coordinates zj (horizontal) versus learned representations z̃i (vertical) for one regime condition.

Figure 12: Structural diagnostics for the nonlinear d = 5 setting with LeakyReLU mixing. All three regime conditions use the same representative seed. Top row: mean absolute encoder Jacobian |Dφ|, where φ = h ◦ g. Bottom row: learned causal graph versus the ground-truth graph.

Figure 13: Latent recovery in the nonlinear d = 7 setting (three-layer MLP, LeakyReLU mixing). Each column shows the 7× 7 scatter matrix of true latent coordinates zj (horizontal) versus learned representations z̃i (vertical) for one regime condition. Under Distinct ratios, each z̃i aligns with exactly one zj . Both controls fail to disentangle.

Figure 14: Structural diagnostics for the nonlinear d = 7 setting with LeakyReLU mixing. All three regime conditions use the same representative seed. Top row: mean absolute encoder Jacobian |Dφ|, where φ = h ◦ g. Bottom row: learned causal graph versus the ground-truth graph.

Table 8: Hardanger data construction for the reported 4–6m/s, 16-channel experiment. The same training tensors are used for the TI-split-regime experiment and for both controls, except that the controls alter or remove the regime labels as described in Section G.2.

Figure 15: Drift-Jacobian diagnostics for a randomly selected seed, shared across the Hardanger regime constructions. Panels follow the main-text order: TI-split regimes (ours), One regime (control), and Shuffled TI labels (control). Rows index drift components f̃ψ,i and columns index
